techniques.evidence_quote: Due to a misconfiguration in the S3 bucket that was hosting the library, a bad actor was able to inject code
someone using the TOR anonymizing network put a modified version of taskrouter.min.js into that path
someone using the TOR anonymizing network put a modified version of taskrouter.min.js into that path
a bad actor was able to inject code that made the user's browser load an extraneous URL
The script returned by the dot-tk URL redirects the user to some other sites, attempts to prevent the user from using their browser back button, and opens new targets transparently.
This script also specifically attempts to gather data about the size of the user's touchscreen and uses events that are targeted at mobile devices.
we believe that this attack was designed to serve malicious advertising to users on mobile devices
this attack appears to be opportunistic and related to a large and well-known campaign to find and exploit open AWS S3 buckets on the Internet for financial gain

attack_chain.description: Attacker scanned the internet for publicly writable AWS S3 buckets as part of a broad opportunistic campaign targeting open S3 buckets.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: this attack appears to be opportunistic and related to a large and well-known campaign to find and exploit open AWS S3 buckets on the Internet for financial gain

attack_chain.description: Attacker exploited a misconfigured S3 bucket policy granting s3:PutObject to Principal '*' (public internet), allowing unauthenticated write access to the TaskRouter SDK path.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: We had not properly configured the access policy for one of our AWS S3 buckets... that meant that anybody on the Internet could read and write to that specific path.

attack_chain.description: Attacker used the TOR anonymizing network to obscure their identity when uploading the malicious file to the S3 bucket.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: someone using the TOR anonymizing network put a modified version of taskrouter.min.js into that path

attack_chain.description: Attacker overwrote the legitimate taskrouter.min.js (v1.20) in the S3 bucket with a maliciously modified version containing injected Magecart JavaScript code.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: A modified version of the TaskRouter JS SDK was uploaded to our site at 1:12 PM PDT... someone using the TOR anonymizing network put a modified version of taskrouter.min.js into that path

attack_chain.description: The injected JavaScript set a tracking cookie (jqueryapi1oad) in victims' browsers and beaconed to an attacker-controlled URL, then loaded a secondary malicious script from a .tk domain targeting mobile device users.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The script that the attacker added sets this cookie in the browser and then makes a request to a URL embedded in the script. (hxxps://gold[.]platinumus[.]top/track/awswrite?q=dmn). That request returns a page with another URL as its only content.

attack_chain.description: The second-stage Magecart script redirected mobile users to malicious advertising sites, suppressed browser back-button navigation, and opened transparent new targets — consistent with malvertising and potential data harvesting.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The script returned by the dot-tk URL redirects the user to some other sites, attempts to prevent the user from using their browser back button, and opens new targets transparently. This script also specifically attempts to gather data about the size of the user's touchscreen.

initial_access_vector: Publicly writable S3 bucket due to misconfigured bucket policy granting s3:PutObject to Principal '*' on the TaskRouter SDK path
persistence_mechanism: Malicious JavaScript injected into the hosted SDK file served via CDN; cached by browsers and CDN for up to 24 hours after remediation
privilege_escalation: None — no privilege escalation required; public s3:PutObject permission was sufficient to overwrite the file
